p.strong: Ctrl+C → Shell Handler → Child Termination via SIGKILL

ul.li: UNIX: kernel sends SIGINT to foreground process group
mCertiKOS: shell receives SIGINT, decides to kill child
Shell is the "signal dispatcher" — simpler kernel design

div.p: Handler can't directly return to kernel. Trampoline code on user stack:
User presses Ctrl+C
Keyboard controller fires IRQ 1 IDT entry 33 → trap() in kernel kbd_intr() reads scancode Translates via ctlmap[] → 0x03 Stores in console buffer
Shell (PID 2) called readline() → sys_readline()
Key insight: Shell itself receives SIGINT, not the child — shell decides what to do
Both processes share CPU via thread_yield():

ul.li: PID 2 (Shell): blocked in sys_readline → getchar → yield

div.p: Timer IRQ fires → thread_yield() → round-robin switch Shell keeps checking console buffer on each timeslice
pending_signals[2] (Shell, PID 2)
Set by sys_readline when it sees 0x03

p.strong: Called in trap() before returning to userspace

div.p: Shell registered a handler via sigaction()!
Modifies user stack to redirect execution:

pre.code: 1. Write trampoline code at user ESP: MOV EAX, 28 // SYS_SIGRETURN INT 0x30 // syscall JMP -2 // safety loop 2. Push: trampoline addr, signum(2), saved_ESP, saved_EIP 3. tf->eip = sa->sa_handler (0x40001dc0) 4. tf->esp = new stack pointer

div.p: When trap returns → CPU jumps to handler!
Shell's handler calls kill(fg_pid, SIGKILL)
SIGKILL cannot be caught or ignored Default action: immediate termination
Child PID 7 is now DEAD Will never be scheduled again
After handler returns, trampoline executes:

pre.code: MOV EAX, 28 // SYS_SIGRETURN INT 0x30 // trap into kernel

div.p: Kernel restores original context:
Shell resumes exactly where it was!
Prints continuously, shares CPU with shell via timesharing
Registered at shell init via: sigaction(SIGINT, &sa) where sa.sa_handler = sigint_handler
Shell tracks which child is "foreground" so Ctrl+C knows what to kill
After sigreturn restores context:

ul.li: fg_pid == 0 → no child to wait for
